segments.title: Three books for interwar naval preparation
segments.summary: A Key Books episode uses three approachable studies to compare how the Royal Navy, U.S. Navy, and Imperial Japanese Navy prepared for war in the 1920s and 1930s.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke frames Andrew Field, Albert Nofi, and Mark Peattie as accessible starting points for understanding the Royal Navy, U.S. Navy, and Japanese naval air power before World War II. The discussion then shows why those books work together: Japanese carrier aviation combined striking power with training and logistics weaknesses, while Royal Navy Far East planning used exercises to test trade defense, Singapore assumptions, and a possible war with Japan.

evidence.note: The opening names the three books and frames them as approachable entry points for the Royal Navy, U.S. Navy, and Japanese Navy in the 1920s and 1930s.
The Sunburst discussion connects Japanese offensive air power to short-war assumptions, aircraft production, training, logistics, and fuel weaknesses.
The Royal Navy strategy section explains Far East exercises, warrant-officer planning problems, trade-defense scenarios, and the gap between fleet plans and Singapore support.
